title: Optimizing Power Factor: A Practical Guide for Factories
summary: Learn how poor power factor impacts electric bills, triggers utility penalties, and how APFC systems restore efficiency.
content: In heavy industries, inductive loads like motors, induction furnaces, and welding equipment consume both active (KW) and reactive (KVAR) power. The ratio of active power to total apparent power is the Power Factor (PF). A low PF indicates inefficient energy consumption, which increases grid line current. Electric utilities penalize factories with PF below 0.90 to cover line load losses. Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) panels solve this by dynamically switching capacitor banks in real-time to match inductive shifts, stabilizing current loops and keeping PF at 0.99. This guide details the financial ROI of capacitive detuned reactor installations in Jaipur's industrial hubs.
author: Amit Kumar

title: Integrating PLC Control Panels with Modern SCADA Frameworks
summary: Explore best practices in linking factory PLC controllers to supervisory control dashboards for live plant telemetry.
content: Factory automation relies on PLC control panels communicating with plant-wide SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) software. Linking these units requires low-latency ethernet fieldbus protocols like Profinet or Modbus TCP. By routing status indicators, motor speeds, and temperature limits from PLC modules to centralized control screens, operators gain real-time visibility. This post reviews hardware isolation, redundant communication networks, and cybersecurity steps for safeguarding industrial control networks against localized line interference.
author: Amit Kumar

title: Why Modern Sub-stations Require Chemical Earthing Systems
summary: Traditional salt and charcoal grids are obsolete. Discover why copper-bonded chemical earthing is the new standard.
content: Grounding systems in high-voltage substations must discharge short-circuits and lightning surges instantly to prevent equipment failure and protect personnel. Traditional pipe earthing using charcoal and salt degrades within a few years as rain flushes the conducting ions away, spiking ground loop impedance. Modern chemical earthing utilizes copper-bonded steel electrodes encased in a moisture-retaining carbon bentonite compound. This eco-friendly backfill expands when damp and does not leach, keeping earth resistance below 1.0 Ohm for over 15 years. We outline design considerations under IEEE 80 compliance guidelines and Rajasthan electricity regulations.
author: Amit Kumar
